content.plain_english: This week in Year 6 maths, your child is working on Converting units. The learning focuses on: solve problems involving the calculation and conversion of units of measure, using decimal notation up to three decimal places where appropriate; recognise that shapes with the same areas can have different perimeters and vice versa; and calculate the area of parallelograms and triangles. These skills build directly on what they have already mastered and prepare them for the next steps in the programme of study.
content.in_school: Children use practical measuring equipment — rulers, scales, measuring cylinders, and clocks — to explore measurement in real contexts. They record findings using diagrams and number lines before progressing to written calculations. Problem-solving tasks show how these skills are used every day.
content.home_activity: Look for real-life examples around the house — a map showing distances, a measuring jug with ml and pints, a food label in grams and kg. Ask your child to read the measurements and convert between them. Road signs in miles are another good context.
dinner_table_questions: Can you show me how to solve problems involving the calculation and conversion of units of measure, using decimal notation up to three decimal places where appropriate?
Was it easy or tricky to recognise that shapes with the same areas can have different perimeters and vice versa? Why?
How would you explain today's maths to someone who has never seen it before?

key_vocabulary.definition: Change a measurement from one unit to another — e.g. 150 cm = 1.5 m.
The modern measurement system using metres, kilograms, litres, etc.
An older system using miles, pounds, pints, etc.
When two measurements have the same value but use different units.
A way of comparing quantities — e.g. 1 kg ≈ 2.2 pounds.

content.times_table_tip: Year 6 children use all their times tables to tackle complex problems including long multiplication, division, and fractions. Challenge them: 'What is 7 × 12?' or 'How many 8s in 72?' Rapid recall frees up thinking space for harder work.
